structured_choices.data_source: EHR records from a single health system (or clearly documented multi-site data with site identifiers retained). 10,000 heart failure patient records, discharge dates 2019–2022. Data includes demographics, clinical history, discharge summaries, and structured clinical variables. All records must have complete discharge date, readmission status follow-up through day 30 post-discharge, and no missing outcome labels.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days post-discharge. Defined as an inpatient admission (not ED-only visit) occurring ≥1 day and ≤30 days after discharge date. Readmission is recorded at time of discharge; no future data beyond day 30 post-discharge is used in any feature or label.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by discharge date: training set uses discharges from 2019–Q2 2021 (approximately 60% of records, ~6,000 patients); validation set uses Q3 2021–Q1 2022 (approximately 20% of records, ~2,000 patients); held-out test set uses Q2–Q4 2022 (approximately 20% of records, ~2,000 patients). Temporal split is chosen because (1) readmission prediction requires causal ordering—the model must not see outcomes of future patients to train; (2) temporal split mimics real deployment where the model must generalize to future patient cohorts; (3) it prevents data leakage from validation/test into training. Within each split, stratification by site (if multi-site) and readmission status ensures balanced hospital representation and class distribution awareness across splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit exclusively on the training set (2019–Q2 2021) and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of statistics from validation/test into the training process. Categorical encoders are fit on training set only; any rare categories in validation/test are handled via a designated 'unknown' category or mapped to the most common class.
structured_choices.features: Raw input signals: (1) Structured features: age, sex, comorbidity flags (diabetes, hypertension, CKD, etc.), ejection fraction, BNP level, discharge medications (ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, diuretic flags), length of stay, prior readmission history (count in past 12 months), social support (living situation encoded). (2) Derived features from discharge summaries (NLP-extracted): presence of documentation of follow-up appointment scheduled, mention of palliative care, documented medication adherence concerns—extracted via keyword/regex or trained NLP model fit on training set only. (3) Temporal features: day of week discharged, season. All features are constructed using only information available at or before discharge; no post-discharge clinical data (labs, vitals, encounters) are included. Features are not post-hoc selected on validation/test; selection is done on training set or informed by domain expertise prior to model training.
structured_choices.model: LSTM + attention ensemble: (a) LSTM component: sequences of clinical events/labs from the 12 months prior to discharge, with attention mechanism to weight temporal dependencies. Input sequence length fixed at 52 weeks (weekly aggregation). (b) Attention mechanism: self-attention layer to identify which prior timepoints contribute most to readmission risk. (c) Ensemble integration: LSTM + attention outputs concatenated with static structured features (demographics, comorbidities) and passed through a 2-layer dense network (64, 32 units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout) to a sigmoid output. Model hyperparameters (LSTM hidden size, attention heads, dense layer sizes, learning rate, dropout rate) are tuned on the validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set as the LSTM (demographics, comorbidities, medications, prior readmission count, social factors, discharge documentation flags). The logistic regression receives identical preprocessing (scaling via StandardScaler, one-hot encoding, imputation via median on training set). Logistic regression hyperparameters (L2 regularization strength, class weight balancing) are tuned on the validation set using grid search over a comparable effort (same validation metric, same number of hyperparameter combinations explored). The baseline is fair because it uses the same feature engineering pipeline, the same train/val/test split, and equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort. It does not include sequential/temporal features (LSTM input) because logistic regression cannot naturally model sequences; this is a justified architectural difference, not a feature asymmetry.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC). Justification: (1) Readmission prediction is inherently a ranking problem—clinicians need to prioritize high-risk patients for intervention; AUROC captures model discrimination across all decision thresholds and is insensitive to class imbalance. (2) AUROC does not require threshold selection a priori, avoiding arbitrary choices. (3) AUROC is standard in clinical prediction literature and facilitates comparison with prior work. (4) The threshold for intervention can be chosen post-hoc based on resource constraints, not baked into the metric.
secondary_metrics: Precision-recall AUC (AUPRC): sensitive to class imbalance; appropriate because readmission is the minority class and cost of false positives (unnecessary interventions) and false negatives (missed high-risk patients) may differ.
Sensitivity and specificity at the operating point chosen for deployment (e.g., 80% sensitivity) to characterize clinical utility.
Calibration (Brier score, calibration plot): ensures predicted probabilities reflect true readmission frequencies so clinicians can interpret model scores as actual risk.
Per-site performance (if multi-site data): AUROC and AUPRC stratified by hospital to detect confounding or interaction with site-level clinical practices.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, regularization, LSTM architecture) is performed on the validation set (Q3 2021–Q1 2022, ~2,000 patients). A grid search or Bayesian optimization routine evaluates candidate hyperparameters by training on the training set and evaluating on the validation set using AUROC as the selection criterion. The test set is not touched during hyperparameter tuning. The final model is retrained on the combined train + validation set (80% of data) using the best hyperparameters, then evaluated once on the held-out test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out and untouched during all development, feature engineering, and hyperparameter tuning. No statistics are computed on the test set until final evaluation. After model selection is complete, the final model (trained on train + validation) is applied to the test set in a single pass. Performance metrics are computed on the test set and reported as the primary evaluation result. The test set is not used for any model selection, debugging, or post-hoc threshold tuning.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Hospital/site effects: If data spans multiple hospitals, site is retained as a stratification variable during train/val/test split to ensure each split represents the same site distribution. Performance is evaluated both overall and per-site to detect site-specific confounding (e.g., differences in discharge practices, follow-up care availability). If site is strongly predictive of readmission independent of clinical features, an interaction term or site-stratified models may be explored post-hoc. Socioeconomic factors: Structured features include proxy variables for social support (living situation, documented discharge planning). These are included in both the baseline and ensemble model, so differences in performance are not confounded by differential access to these features. Temporal drift: The temporal split by discharge date inherently accounts for potential changes in clinical practice, hospital processes, or patient population over time. Performance is reported per year (2022 test set only) to detect if the model's performance degrades over time. Imbalanced class distribution: Training uses class weights (balanced mode in loss function) to weight minority class (readmitted) more heavily, ensuring the model does not default to always predicting 'no readmission.' Both baseline and ensemble use class weighting, ensuring fair comparison. Scope and claims: This design establishes that the ensemble model has better discrimination (AUROC) than logistic regression on this specific health system's 2022 discharge cohort. It does NOT establish generalizability to other hospitals, other patient populations (non-heart failure), or other time periods. Separate temporal or external validation studies would be required for those claims.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: LSTM + Attention vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy
The experiment uses the full 10,000-patient EHR dataset spanning 2019–2022, with discharge date as the temporal anchor. The outcome label is binary readmission within 30 days: defined as an inpatient admission occurring ≥1 day and ≤30 days after discharge. Only information available at discharge (prior history, discharge medications, discharge summary documentation) is used as features; no post-discharge clinical data is permitted to prevent information leakage. Records with missing outcome labels or incomplete follow-up through day 30 are excluded prior to any analysis.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split
A temporal split by discharge date is employed to reflect real-world deployment and prevent causal leakage:
- **Training set:** Discharges from 2019–Q2 2021 (~6,000 patients, 60%)
- **Validation set:** Discharges from Q3 2021–Q1 2022 (~2,000 patients, 20%)
- **Test set:** Discharges from Q2–Q4 2022 (~2,000 patients, 20%)

Within each split, the data is stratified by hospital site (if multi-site) and readmission status to ensure balanced representation of both hospitals and outcome classes. Temporal split is justified because readmission prediction is causal: the model must not see outcomes from future patient cohorts during training. This prevents contamination and mimics how the model will be deployed prospectively.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
All features are constructed from information available at discharge. The feature set includes:
- **Static structured features:** Age, sex, comorbidity indicators (diabetes, hypertension, chronic kidney disease), ejection fraction, BNP level, discharge medications (ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, diuretic flags), length of stay, and 12-month prior readmission count.
- **Documentation features (from discharge summary):** Binary indicators for scheduled follow-up appointment, palliative care mention, and medication adherence concerns, extracted via keyword matching on training data.
- **Temporal features:** Day of week and season at discharge.

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for numeric features, OneHotEncoder for categorical features, SimpleImputer for missing values) are fit exclusively on the training set. Fitted transformers are applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents validation and test statistics from leaking into training.

### 4. Baseline and Model Specifications
**Baseline Model:** Logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, comorbidities, discharge medications, prior readmission count, social factors, documentation flags). The baseline receives identical preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning effort as the ensemble. Class weights are applied to both models to account for class imbalance.

**Ensemble Model:** An LSTM + attention architecture that combines temporal sequential information with static features:
1. LSTM component: Sequences of clinical events from the 12 months prior to discharge (weekly aggregation, sequence length = 52 weeks) are passed through an LSTM layer (tunable hidden size).
2. Attention mechanism: A self-attention layer weights the contribution of each timepoint, identifying which prior events are most predictive of readmission.
3. Integration: LSTM + attention outputs are concatenated with static structured features and passed through a 2-layer dense network (64, 32 units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout) to a sigmoid output.

Hyperparameters for both models (regularization, learning rate, LSTM hidden size, dropout) are tuned on the validation set using grid search, with AUROC as the selection criterion.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set only. The best-performing model (by AUROC on validation) is identified, then both the baseline and ensemble are retrained on the combined train + validation data (80%) using the selected hyperparameters. Final evaluation is performed on the test set in a single pass; the test set is never touched during tuning or development.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC). AUROC is chosen because readmission prediction is a ranking task—clinicians prioritize high-risk patients for intervention. AUROC is insensitive to class imbalance and does not require a priori threshold selection, allowing flexibility in deployment.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): Captures performance where readmission is the minority class.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant operating points (e.g., 80% sensitivity).
- Brier score and calibration plots: Ensure predicted probabilities reflect true readmission frequencies.
- Per-site AUROC and AUPRC: Detect whether hospital-level practices confound results.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope
**Hospital/site effects:** If data is multi-site, stratification during splitting ensures each split reflects the same hospital distribution. Performance is evaluated per-site to detect confounding by clinical practice differences.

**Socioeconomic factors:** Proxy variables (living situation, discharge planning documentation) are included in both models, ensuring fair comparison.

**Temporal drift:** The temporal test set (2022 only) reveals whether model performance changes over time.

**Class imbalance:** Class weighting in the loss function ensures both models appropriately weight the minority (readmitted) class.

**Scope:** This experiment establishes that the ensemble model has better discrimination than logistic regression on this health system's 2022 discharge cohort. It does not establish generalizability to other hospitals or populations; external validation would be required for those claims.
